title: SCOPE: A Syndrome-Driven Control Plane for QEC-Enabled Quantum Networks
authors: Xiaojie Fan, Zian Wang, Ashutosh Tiwari, Himanshu Gupta
abstract: As quantum networks evolve from experimental testbeds to fault-tolerant systems, the primary performance metric shifts from physical link fidelity to end-to-end logical error rate. However, current control planes remain ill-equipped for this transition: routing decisions are typically decoupled from Quantum Error Correction (QEC) strategies, relying on topology or scalar fidelity metrics that fail to predict how specific physical noise structures interact with logical codes. Optimizing this coupled route-and-code performance requires precise, real-time visibility into network error biases, yet traditional active tomography is operationally prohibitive due to throughput collapse and service interruption.   We present SCOPE (Syndrome-based COntrol PlanE), a network-layer architecture that enables joint routing and coding optimization using purely passive telemetry. Instead of injecting probes, SCOPE harvests error syndromes -- the parity-check outcomes naturally generated by QEC decoders during user service. By aggregating these signals, SCOPE's inference engine reconstructs the network's time-varying error map, capturing complex, context-dependent noise correlations. This visibility drives a decision engine that proactively pushes optimal route-and-code configurations to source nodes. NetSquid and IBM-calibrated simulations show that SCOPE reduces estimation error by more than 60% relative to a standard EM baseline. In large-scale networks, this precision reduces logical error rates by 30-35% (up to 65%) against topology-aware baselines.
summary: This paper introduces SCOPE, a novel control plane for fault-tolerant quantum networks that jointly optimizes network routing and QEC code selection. By passively analyzing error syndromes generated during normal operation, it reconstructs the true, time-varying noise map of the network. This allows it to select optimal paths and codes in real-time, achieving substantial reductions in logical error rates over existing methods.
main_problem: Current quantum network control planes fail because they treat routing decisions and Quantum Error Correction (QEC) code selection as decoupled processes, leading to suboptimal end-to-end logical error rates.
main_result: The proposed SCOPE architecture significantly reduces estimated error by over 60% and lowers logical error rates by 30-35% in large networks compared to topology-aware baselines.
method: SCOPE uses passive syndrome tomography, harvesting parity-check outcomes from live user traffic to build a time-varying network error map, which then drives a joint optimization engine for route and code selection.
model_or_system: The system models quantum networks as graphs where information is protected by QEC. The methodology supports both direct transmission (pathing) and teleportation-based communication (swap trees).
key_observables: End-to-End Logical Error Rate (LER), syndrome histograms, Pauli error rate vectors ($\vec{p}$), and the inferred network error map $\hat{\Theta}$.
important_parameters: The performance is sensitive to noise biases (e.g., $p_X, p_Y, p_Z$) and path history dependence, requiring joint optimization over route and code.
main_assumptions: It assumes that passive syndrome data can provide sufficient statistical information to reconstruct the complex, time-varying error map without interrupting service or injecting probes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SCOPE system overview following an SDN pattern, showing how the Tomography Engine infers $\hat{\Theta}$ and the Decision Engine selects optimal $(	ext{Route}, 	ext{Code})$ tuples to minimize LER.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the need for joint optimization (decoupling flaw), introduces SCOPE's passive syndrome tomography approach, details the DST/GNN inference engine, and concludes with performance validation across various error regimes (direct vs. teleportation).
why_interesting: While focused on networking protocols, the core technique of using deep learning to invert complex physical measurements (syndromes) into actionable system parameters is highly relevant for modeling open quantum systems where full state tomography is impossible.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: SCOPE System Overview. The architecture follows a closed-loop Software-Defined Networking (SDN) pattern. The Con- trol Plane hosts the intelligence, comprising the Learning Engine (which infers the Error Map ˆΘ from passive syndromes) and the Decision Engine (which computes optimized Route-Code plans). The Data Plane executes these plans, transmitting qubits, generating remote entanglement, and exporting decoding-syndrome outcomes as feedback. (biases) and (2) cheap enough to collect continuously.
